title: 2025/12/04 - JU (Part 2)
displayTitle: 2025-12-04 - Justice Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Justice [Part 2]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Justice
description: Thursday 4 December 2025 | Justice Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Justice Submitters: Ministry of Justice Andrew Kibblewhite, Chief Executive
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Justice. Ministry of Justice. Andrew Kibblewhite, Chief Executive

chunks.text: Oh. Yeah. (laughs) It's a week past. We haven't seen you, have we? Lawrence, where have you been? Hi. You've been- Oh, well, we're having a ball. (laughing) We all have the same questions. Yeah, okay. Haven't been taken in yet. (laughs) Oh.

I used to live in Takanini. Where? On Te Reinga Drive right by Omston, before that all got developed. And that used to be my electorate, Lawrence. Takanini? No, Omston area. Omston. Ah. Well, Judith, our prime minister's given him... No. So it's gone from me, to Judith, to the Prime Minister. And is... But Jenny's turned up. Oh, you've got a lot of laboring people.

(laughing) Hang I don't usually vote for them. Right. When's, when's Colin? Mm-hmm. (laughs) Yeah. When are you going home? Five. Go on, Greg, sit down to there. Hello. (laughs) We get it right. No. Better than this chair. I don't want to be rude but she's a permanent address, even if they're not.

Right, Mr. Kivawai, we are on very strict timeframe. You know we've got a structured agenda. We try to roughly keep to it. Apparently you've got six minutes, no more- (laughs)... w- which is expensive. But, uh, anyway, um- Nothing interesting to say. Welcome to you and your team. I'll let you introduce everyone but, um, the floor is yours.

Kia ora e nga mana o te mema o te pana mata. Kei kua whakaroahia mai nei tēnā tautou. Kia ora tatou katoa. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Uh, let me very quickly just introduce some of my team. I've got quite a full group here but I'll, I'll just only name those who I'm pretty confident will get called upon to support me over the next couple of hours. Uh, on my left, you will have met Victoria Davy in this committee before. She's our new, uh, uh Dep Sec Aoteaāringa Strategy.

Yeah. Uh, Andy Fullbrook, our CFO, is an old friend- Mm-hmm.... of this, uh, group. Uh, over here we have Ryan Orange, who runs the Sector Directorate, and Becks Parish, who will talk about numbers, if you have detailed question on numbers. And these three, um, uh, attractive looking people here- (laughs)... (laughs) are Dr Kelvin Watson, who's our Dep Sec, uh, Corporate- Can we get a chair?

Uh, Karl Craifer, who is the Chief Operating Officer, and Victoria McLachlan, who's the Dep Sec for the Digital, uh, Transformation Waioraika program. And there are others here who I will, uh, introduce if I need to. Caroline Greany, our Dep Sec Policy apologizes. She'll be a little bit late- Okay.

... but, uh, she's rushing, uh, back. Uh, and I thank the committee. I think you indulged us in- That's fine. ... changing the agenda so that she could attend her daughter's prize giving. Yes. Just administratively while, while it's... If you, if you need to come up, don't... Just wait. Come up and talk to the microphone. Just let's not have that show of standing back. Just come up seamlessly, right?

(laughs) Okay. Sorry to interrupt. No, I think, Mr. Chair, my six minutes probably starts now. (laughing) So... No. My rec, you've had 43 seconds. (laughing) Look, thank you for this opportunity. Uh, obviously we look forward to it, uh, all year. Um, at the Ministry of Justice- (laughs)... our purpose- You said that with such conviction.

(laughs) Yeah, no sarcasm. (laughs) Slice and celly. Our purpose is to strengthen people's trust in the law and this does guide, uh, what we do every day. Uh, this year we refreshed our statement of intent, uh, to provide a clearer view of our longer term outcomes and priorities for the next four years, uh, to ensure alignment with change in government priorities and outline specific activities to achieve those outcomes and priorities. Uh, our updated statement of intent identifies three outcomes we believe define a just soc- society from, uh, our

perspective. Firstly, uh, trusted and legitimate, where people believe in the fairness and independence of the law. Uh, secondly, a justice system that is accessible and effective, where justice isn't delayed and where everyone, regardless of means, can participate meaningfully. And thirdfl- uh, thirdly, a safe and prosperous society where people feel secure and the law supports both social cohesion and economic confidence. Overall, these key strategic objectives remain consistent with what we've been working towards over the past five years.

And they're not abstract ideas. We want them to be lived realities for all New Zealanders. We've had a really busy year in the ministry supporting the government's focus on the justice system and law and order, uh, noting its recent announcement that it is tracking well ahead of the, uh, violent... victims of violent crime target, reducing that. Uh, we're also working hard to improve timeliness in the courts and improve our digital and physical infrastructure, and I'm sure we'll talk more about that this afternoon.

Uh, and obviously we are managing in a constrained fiscal environment and that does color quite a lot of our world. Alongside our justice partners, uh, and the judiciary, we are tackling court backlogs, recognizing the real effect that these have on victims, defendants, and other parties. Uh, the national backlog of criminal cases in the District Court, uh, has fallen by 27% since February '24, which is the equivalent of around 2,200 cases. Uh, active coronial cases have decreased by 19% in the year to October, uh, from

five and a half thousand to four and a half thousand cases. In the Disputes Tribunal, an extra 1,200 were completed in the 12 months to October compared to the previous year. Uh, these examples show we can make progress on delays and backlogs. It's felt like an intractable challenge at times, but we're, uh, really, I think, demonstrating that we can get some traction, uh, in it. But none of it is achieved in isolation.

It's a testament to the collective will of everyone involved. Uh, we've also ramped up our fines and reparations collection effort. In '24-'25, uh, $223 million was collected. That's 50 dol- $50 million more than in 2022, uh, '23. And as a result of that, outstanding reparation money owed to victims has dropped from, uh, 111.8 million in the year to June '24 to 104.6 million this year. Our policy team has been incredibly busy. We've supported the government with sentencing reform, reinstated the three strikes regime, we worked on coward punch offending, intro- introduced a new

stalking offense. Measures to tackle retail crime have been progressed following advice from the Ministerial Advisory Group for Victims of Retail Crime. On cybercrime, we've worked to ensure New Zealand meets the requirements of the Budapest Convention, and we're currently leading an overhaul of the country's anti-money laundering and countering the financing of terrorism system.

Two phases, (clears throat) excuse me, of the government's firearms reform program have been delivered, improvements to firearms prohibition orders and rule changes for shooting clubs and ranges. Work is advancing on the Arms Act rewrite, and we're helping to move the Firearms Safety Authority to a new independent regulatory agency. In the civil and family law space, we have ensured small straightforward estates can be distributed without needing High Court approval for probate. We've strengthened the Family Court's ability to address litigation

abuse, and we've supported the government to pass urgent legislation temporarily suspending unsafe overseas adoptions. We supported this committee's consideration of the Treaty Principles Bill, including analysis of an unprecedented volume of public submissions, and we've developed options to improve electoral system operations.

On the infrastructure front, we're tackling long-standing issues with both our digital and physical infrastructure. We're replacing the court's, uh, paper-based system with a digital case management system, uh, Te Oreka, uh, and we're currently in the middle, uh, or nearing the end of the build phase for the Family Court. And there's an extensive property work program underway with several major rebuilds and other significant projects.

Uh, we've just finished consulting and are about to finalize our latest Long Term Insights Briefing. Our topic is the future operation of the courts and justice services. It would be good to have an opportunity to discuss that with this committee, uh, in the new year, if you desire that. Uh, and, uh, clearly, uh, we're just one part of the justice sector. Uh, we work alongside the other justice sector agencies, Police, Corrections, Crown Law, Serious Fraud Office, and Oranga Tamariki. We understand our interdependencies on

timeliness, the victim experience, and our infrastructure. I'm really appreciative of the sense of shared endeavor that we have with those other agencies. Uh, I would want to put on the record, I'm incredibly proud of the work of all our staff, our kaimahi. Uh, this past year, uh, we have achieved much, uh, under a tight fiscal situation. I acknowledge our ministers, I acknowledge the judiciary for their leadership and support.

And of course, I acknowledge this committee, uh, for the conversations that we have on a regular basis. And- What a wonderful thing to hear.... I'm in your hands, Mr. Chair. Thank you. Right. I have got Mr. Todd Stevens. Todd, Thanks. We're, we're, we're into the audit findings- Mm-hmm.... section of the structured agenda. And I'm just referring to the, to the letter from the Audit Office to your, uh, ministers, which is great.

Um, and look, I know your, you've got a very complex department. Um, you seem to be stuck in the good zone now on the three audits, but you can give me some comments on that in a minute. But one thing I did want to ask, obviously, I think, uh, in both this audit and maybe subsequent audits, there has been some comments around, um, the, your IT control environment and your procurement processes. So, keen just for you to give us a bit more, um, the committee a bit more insight into how you have been addressing some of those

issues, 'cause I think that they've been raised a few times, and obviously you've made progress, but maybe more to do. Yeah. Um, uh, certainly it might be worth, uh, uh, Dr. Watson joining us at the, at the table for, for, for some of this. Mm-hmm. Uh, look, in, in both of those, uh, spaces, in the, in the digital space, uh, to be honest, I, I think we have been working through a legacy of underinvestment.

Uh, that underinvestment, uh, had meant that a lot of our systems, uh, were out of service, uh, that they didn't fit or work well together. And this is something that, to be honest, has occurred probably over the l- you know, the last 15, uh, years. Uh, we've, we've, uh, done a lot of work, have a very active program to try and bring that, uh, up to speed.

Uh, in 2022, uh, we, uh, had a, uh, a budget that recognized some of that deficit, uh, and that helped us, uh, get ahead. So we are progressively going through and, and making sure that we both, um, uh, replace or, or close down some old systems, some legacy systems, uh, but we ensure others are, are well, uh, supported.

Across the top of that, we've obviously got, uh, Te Oreka, uh, the digital case management system, uh, coming in. That, that will be a complete change, uh, game-changer for us in the courts.... uh, and so we're looking forward to that. Uh, procurement, I might actually get, uh, Kelvin to talk a little bit about what we've done in the procurement space.

Uh, in particular, we have, uh, it's, it's one of the areas where we've been using, uh, AI to, to get a better sense of just the state of our contracts and where they're at. And I thought, Kelvin, you might want to make any, m- any other comments about digital as, as well, but you might want to talk about that, some of that work in procurement. Sure. Um, so in the procurement space, we've got...

we don't have a fully end-to-end contract management system, um, to enable us to, to manage, uh, contracts in a, in a very good sense. Uh, but we do have a contract database where we capture all our contracts. And what we've been doing more recently is using lots of AI, uh, agents to help us, um, basically bring together all of the documentation that we've got.

So, if we've got a contract, uh, it's been a long-standing contract, a series of variations, what the AI agents will help us do is actually go through all of those documents and then tell us what are the... what's the set of current, uh, contract clauses for that contract, taking all the variations. So, we've got a number of things that we're doing, um, that are helping us to, uh, make sure that we're robust in our, in our commercial services practices, uh, while not having a, a fully entailed, um, contract management system.

And just a quick couple of sups then, and obviously we're going to talk about, um, the RF a little later. That's obviously- Mm-hmm.... a big project. But just, just so we get a sense, I mean, how many other kind of technology systems is the ministry managing? You must have many, many. I mean... Uh, I, I'd need to, um, come back to the committee the exact numbers, but I think we, we record something around 60 or 70 what we call critical systems.

Yeah. Just like our case management system, our finance system, payroll system, that sort of thing. So there's about 70 odd that we would consider critical, um, and then there's a further, um, that's in the hundreds in terms of just various systems that we have, uh, that support, uh, the overall management of, of the ministry's activities.

And so what we do is we, uh, we have particular focus on those 60 or 70 critical systems in terms of the, um, certification accreditation, those making sure security and privacy settings are robust. We've still got work to do in that space so they're not, um, fully all, um, certified and accredited and we've got remediations even on those critical ones. Um, but we've continued to improve year on year.

We're seeing, um, improved, um, assurance around all of those critical systems. And just one final one, just around procurement. Well, two parts to it. Yeah, one, are you looking at an end-to-end contract management system or do you think that this AI technology you're kind of putting over the top is going to be enough? And again, just, yeah, what kind of, um, insights is that giving you and how are you acting on them, I suppose?

Yeah, um, this is probably two parts. So in terms of our, um, you know, we would like to get all of our systems too. We'd love to have a contract management system. We'd love to move to full procure-to-pay, um, process and, and system integration of that. Um, but that has to be prioritised against everything else that we're doing in the ministry, all of the, um, other technology challenges that we have. And actually, we're, we're reasonably comfortable at this stage that while we'd like to do those things, that actually the things we are focused on are

things like how we manage our, our legal aid debt, um, obviously the Te Ureka program, that those need to be the priorities and that we've got the priorities right. Um, and so what our focus has been on is the absence of kind of those, like, I guess, best pr- best practice systems in that, um, commercial space, procurement space, is making sure that we're putting in the processes that enable us to be assured that even without a contract management system, w- we're, we're okay and we've got the right checks and balances in place.

Um, and so, um, in that I gave one example- Mm-hmm.... of how we're using AI. There's actually a number of ways in which we're using AI, even through, um, some of our procurement process, our contract management process. Um, and so that is, that is giving us, um... What the AI is doing effectively is it's doing a lot of the manual work- Yes.... and so our procurement team are actually able to spend more time on the value-adding activities, which is if the AI can do that, that manual stuff around what actually is the most up-to-date version of contract, then they, they're

using that to then, um, add value in terms of, "Well, what's the contract saying? How do we work with the supplier?" And that sort of thing. Thank you. Okay, now we're meant to be moving to just outcomes for Māori, but, um, Dr. Sunan, you had a question? Just, just outcomes for Māori? You've got those ****** out. Oh. We have a str- New agenda. ... new agenda. Okay. Uh, no, I'm, I'm okay. I had some- Do you want to talk about the order?

Okay. No, no, we don't want- Do you want to talk about the order? No, no, no. We'll go with this order. Thank you. All right. So- I have questions later, then. Okay. Who's up for a question? I can certainly ask a question. Right, Honorable Dr. Duncan Webb. I'm just actually, uh, interested in a really, um, global question, which I, I've always kind of struggled to understand. Because you've got a number of programs in place across the organization which seek to address issues with Māori and the justice system, um, but I'm not quite sure

how we know when we've, when we are succeeding, um, and certainly when we have succeeded. Um, so can you just talk about what measures would be in place to say, "We are on track." And I know it's aspirational, but how would we know when we've arrived?

Well, look, that's a, I think, a really good question because it, it helps us locate back in the statement of intent, back in the outcomes that we've got and where our purpose sits. So, our, our purpose statement says we want to en- en- strengthen people's trust in the, in the law. Uh, we monitor and we've put into the, uh, crime and victim survey now questions that specifically address what is the levels of trust in the law.

And we can break that down by different groups, including by Māori, uh, and others. So we can... and we know that there's a gap there, that Māori levels of trust in the justice system are lower than, uh, non-Māori. So there's a metric that we can manage there. We, we have six priority areas, you know, that we have set for ourselves. One of them is reducing disproportionality of Māori, uh, in the, in the, in the court system and we've particularly decided to, to track that at the-... at the, uh, number of Māori, uh, appearing,

uh, who are coming into court. Yes. Uh, and I think- Coming for the first time, do you mean? Beg your pardon? Coming to court for the first time? Oh, no, no, not for the first time. Just generally? However often they come to court, 'cause actually that's helpful because it picks up, uh, where there is, uh, re-offending, uh, and the like, which I think is also part of the dynamic here and part of what you need to solve to get on top of, of some of these issues.

So, I think those numbers, um, uh, you know, we know that for the general, uh, population of, of Māoris about 17 or 18%, I think, uh, the offending levels or first appearance in court is, uh, 41%, uh, for Māori. So, so we, we can and do, and are quite active in tracking some of those things there.

And the other one that you haven't mentioned, which you might want to come back to, is victim, Māori victims, right? Absolutely, yeah. Um, do, and are those numbers tracking positively or are they s- uh, my understanding was they're pretty static. They are pretty static in the, in the court space. Some of the numbers are, the absolute numbers are coming down, but, but, but because numbers of category two cases coming into the courts are going down, that trend is actually what's happening.

Uh, the work, there's a, there's a story in here about the workload of the court. There are more category three cases coming in, which is a lot more intense. Overall numbers of people coming into court are just dropping slightly and that's because the category two numbers are going down. Māori is going down, but so, so are the rest. And you're right, that we're not making any particular ground on the question of disproportionality.

Mm-hmm. So, how are you responding to the fact that you're not making progress? Well, so we have a range of, of, of, of projects in the space to be, uh, measuring is really important and, and keeping it to the forefront. Uh, there are a number of particular programs, some of them which are not just oriented at Māori- I think, I, I, you know, I've had a look at the programs, and, and you're right, there's plenty in, in strategies and so on, but most of them appear to have been in place for quite some time.

Um, I, I can't, and you might be able to inform me, I can't see any new programs or programs which are taking a new approach. And given that we've got a static situation, the question is what are we doing differently that's going to move the needle in the right direction? Well, uh, the first comment I'd make on that is, you know, what we see in our court system is the back end of a big system of, of often disadvantage, uh, and the like.

So, we're always gonna be a bit of a receiver. There are levers that we can pull in the court system and in the justice system- Funnily enough, corrections said exactly the same thing. Well, they're even further down the line, Well, I know, but you see the point, right? That if we had, we have a justice cluster for a reason, but anyway ... No, no, but I, well, I, I would put all of us in the justice cluster and I mean, I, I think there's absolutely leaders and I'll talk about a few other things that we're working on there. But we're also a product of broader

socioeconomic, of there's things happening in the benefit system or the education system, or, or other sorts of things. So, I'm not, I'm not, I'm not in any way absolving us from the responsibility to tackle this. I'm just saying, to some extent, you know, what, you know, I wouldn't set a very specific target for us in some of these numbers for Māori because there are so many things well beyond our control.

I track it and keep asking the question, whether we're doing enough. To answer your question about whether, uh, whether we are doing things that are new and that we can see a change on, the, to measure these sorts of outcomes takes quite a long time. So, there's actually, over the last three or four years, there has been a lot of new activity in this space. There's the Young Adults List Courts, there is Te Omarama, which is, neither of these are just oriented towards Māori, but because there are a lot of Māori in the population and they're in, in

the courts, they have a big part of the impact there. But Te Omarama is about making the court experience, it's more likely to bring a therapeutic angle, it's more likely to offer solutions. The Young Adults List Courts are about recognizing the needs of individuals. We've, we're running a, a couple of programs, uh- So, as your response, 'cause I, I, you know, I've read the documents and you've got a number of other proj- uh, programs as well.

Are, are you essentially saying these programs are long-term programs, and it's simply too early to tell? On some of those we have some indications, but, um, and I'm, um, Becs might want to come up and, and talk about some of this, uh, but if you're talking about re-offending, you know, you're, you're talking re-offending in one year, re-offending in two years.

If, by the time you launch something, you're, you're immediately in sort of three years before you're really gonna have a clear sense of whether something like that's happened. So, so- But the, the danger is we get into a ten-year change cycle, right? Something not working has been in place for 10 years before you revisit it. It's not very nimble. Well, I think there's a question of being nimble- So-... and there's a question of just how long some things take.

Yeah, okay, so... Mm-hmm. I'm gonna- Okay. I'm sorry, you go. Sorry. And you're after. Hi, I was just gonna, uh, speak to, speak to Leonid's question- Yeah.... a little bit. Um, so we have been doing this year a, a number of outcomes reviews, uh, looking at particular therapeutic courts, um, to understand what some of the outcomes related to sentencing, re-offending, timeliness, et cetera.

Um, that's still, uh, you know, we're in a relatively infant space, but we've been, um, able to see particularly s- for the Rangitahi courts, uh, that those, uh, Rangitahi that go through those courts, 75% of them will complete their family group plan and be, um, discharged without conviction. Um, and that's in comparison to those that go through the normal youth court at, at a rate of 60%. So, in terms of reducing disproportionality and of conviction, uh, we are seeing some success in that space. In terms of Te Omarama, it's really only been rolled out,

uh, in a full implementation state at the moment, but we've put a lot of effort into establishing the base measures and also setting up ways that we can track and monitor progress ahead of being able to give you a very clear, uh, understanding of the longer term impacts of that. And so there's both a monitoring framework and an evaluation framework that sits behind that. But in reality, uh, when someone goes through the court system...That in itself takes some time, especially if you're taking a therapeutic approach. Then they will have to have at least

18 months out the other side before we'd even be able to start to look at the, sort of re-offending rates. So for a lot- I support that. ... of this stuff it's very- We've got a couple of subs. Right. So, um, Ms. Wolgers and then I'm going to Rima and then I'm going to new um- One- one second. Thank you. Thank you, Mr.- Mine's also kind of Thank you, Mr. Chair, and thank you Secretary and to your staff- Oh, hey, Alan. ... across the country. Um, I suppose there's an attached question of whether the performance measures themselves are sufficient.

So I note that in relation to- Mm-hmm.... legal aid for example, the performance measure appears to be the number of legal aid cases that are granted, or cases that are granted legal aid, as opposed to looking at quality, resolution, um, and response. And so is there an effort to look at the measures themselves and whether there ought to be additional measures?

We, we have a huge number of measures that we track and that we look at across all parts of our business. And, and we're forever pressurizing Bex and her team to take some new, new takes on the material so we can understand it, uh, better. In legal aid there's a lot of different things that go on there. We, we have measurements I think Carl may want to m- come up and speak to this, but we would, we have, we have measures around, uh, um, that, that go to the, you know and processing of approvals, to the, uh, the, the payments, the, the numbers of, of, of

re-applications and the like. It's, they don't specifically go to the quality of the legal advice 'cause that's a, that's, that's a, it's a different bit of a ballpark than we're really able to do, uh, much about. But, but I, I think in terms of the measures, we're always looking at how we can, um, sharpen up our measures and, and stay on top of things.

Yeah. We've got legal aid coming up at 2:50. Um, I want to go back to Rima. We were just talking about Māori th- um- Yeah. Sorry? I just want to confirm it... Hello. Thank you for coming in again. Um, I just want to confirm that Māori victimization as mentioned in the, um, report on page 25 has gone down from 39% in 2018 to 34% in 2024. Yep, still slow but it's- Yeah.

... yeah, it's trending down- Yeah.... um, which is a positive. It is. Yeah. Just confirming that. It is. Yeah. Mm-hmm. No. Thank you, thank you for, um, noting that. Yeah. Okay. Uh, I'm coming back to Honorable Ginny Anderson. Well, thank you very much. Um, I, I was looking at some data recently that showed an increase, um, of, particularly around prosecutions for personal use of cannabis, uh, was really dis- had gone up over, over the past sort of 12, 18 months.

And, and that Māori were really disproportionately affected, um, in, in those statistics. A- and it was qui- a, a bit of a jump from what we'd seen in the past. I, I'm, I'm really interested to understand from a justice perspective why you see an increase in those, those prosecutions in that particular area. A- and furthermore, whether you think there's any systemic bias that's causing, um, Māori to be disproportionately represented?

I, I, I think that's a, that's a really interesting question and I've, and my answer will be one of, um, uh, examining possibilities 'cause I don't think we, we fully understand the reasons behind that. But the, in some of the changes that have come through in, in recent legislation there has been an extension of the ability for searches, certainly the gang patches in- increased the number of searches and there are, I think there are other instances where the, the police have more search powers than they did. I mean one, and it's a

hypothesis rather than something I can, um, put my hand on heart and say, is that it's, it's quite likely that because there are more searches happening now, there will likely be more drugs found and that has seen an increase in the possession charges for both cannabis and methamphetamine. Mm-hmm. Do, do, do you have a view like that, that has been systemically one that has been, uh, disproportionately represented by Māori.

One of the, for Māori, one of the views is, is that, um, if there's another crime that's potentially been committed and a person is suspected of committing that crime, they're turned over and found a small amount of cannabis and held on that basis until further work is done to understand whether the secondary offenses are able to be prosecuted or not.

So cannabis can be used as a, as a purpose of holding somebody for further investigation. Are you aware of that practice occurring? I, I'm, I'm, I'm not aware of that practice. You'd have to ask police about that. I wouldn't be able to offer any insight on that one. But the change to the Misuse of Drugs Act that was I think in 2019, it actually asked for there to be a presumption of a personal use for a referral to a health or a treatment service as opposed to prosecution.

Why do you think we've moved away from a health based response for personal users of, of low levels of cannabis? A- again, you're really talking about decisions that often police will be making and you'd have to address that question to police. I, I don't, I, I don't have a perspective to offer on that one. But a- a- as a cluster, with, with, with a focus on reducing disproportionate representation of Māori within our justice system, do you not have an eye over some areas that are changing and- We, we absolutely-... can

ask those questions? ... could get to that question. That's, it's not, it's, it's, uh, to be honest some of this, the, the, the impact, uh, or the increase in the number of drugs charges is, is, uh, uh, I've, it's become part of the conversations I'm having only quite recently. But I think some of the work we're doing would absolutely ask the question about what's driving that. And if we understand what's driving that, uh, that would lead to a justice sector conversation.

But we haven't had that sort of conversation at this point. And, and you have no kind of theory or view as to... You, you just think it might be the, the changes to some of the gang stuff? Uh- But that was only a short time ago. That's quite a short... But, well, true but that's, that's, that's- Nine months. I'd, I'd, I'd want to test that harder and it's a, it's a worthwhile, uh, piece of work to do.

Okay. You've got something? Yes, uh, just a few questions on, um, some of the stuff that you already mentioned. I think the first one is around Te Ao Mārama.
